coredata.dc:title: Ultrasound energy to accelerate dye uptake and dye–fiber interaction of reactive dye on knitted cotton fabric at low temperatures
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Tissera, Nadeeka D.
Wijesena, Ruchira N.
de Silva, K.M. Nalin
coredata.dc:description: Acoustic cavitation formed due to propagation of ultrasound wave inside a dye bath was successfully used to dye cotton fabric with a reactive dye at lower temperatures. The energy input to the system during sonication was 0.7W/cm2. This was within the energy range that contributes towards forming cavitation during ultra-sonication. The influence of ultrasound treatment on dye particle size and fiber morphology is discussed. Particle size analysis of the dye bath revealed ultra-sonication energy was capable of de-agglomeration of hydrolyzed dye molecules during dyeing. SEM micrograph and AFM topographical image of the fiber surface revealed fiber morphology remains unchanged after the sonication. The study was extended in understanding the contribution of ultrasound method of dyeing towards achieving good color strength on the fabric, compared to the normal heating method of dyeing. Study showed color strength obtained using ultra sound method of dyeing is higher compared to normal heating dyeing. Ultrasound energy was able to achieve the good color strength on cotton fabric at very low temperature such as 30°C, which was approximately 230% more than the color strength achieved in normal heating method of dyeing. This indicates that energy input to the system using ultrasound was capable of acting as an effective alternative method of dyeing knitted cotton fabrics with reactive dye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Cotton
Knitted
Cavitation
Reactive dye

Physiochemical characterization of the cotton fabrics
Energy input to the dye bath during sonication
Dye particle size analysis during dyeing
Analysis of dye fiber interaction at different stages of dyeing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Chemical structure of the Reactive yellow dye. (b) Mechanism of dye attachment to the cellulose fiber.

Apparatus for ultra sound dyeing experiment (1) convertor, (2) disrupter horn, (3) probe, (4) dye bath, (5) water tank, (6) temperature sensor, (7) cooling controller, and (8) stainless steel dye vessel.
Percentage of dye exhaustion (a) after first 30min of dyeing. (b) After 60min of dyeing.
Dye fixation on the fabric (a) after first 30min of dyeing. (b) After 60min of dyeing.
Dye bath particle size (a) after 30min dyeing. (b) After 60min dyeing.

Schematic presentation of dye fiber interaction and dye particle size distribution of dye bath during normal heating method of dyeing (a) during first 30min of dyeing at 60°C. (b) After the addition of NaCO3 to dye bath and dyeing for 60min at 60°C.
FT-IR spectrum obtained for (a) normal cotton, (b) cotton fabric dyed with normal heating at 30°C, and (c) cotton fabric dyed with ultra sound at 30°C.

Scanning electron microscope images of (a) untreated cotton fiber, (b) untreated cotton fiber surface, (c) dyed cotton fiber using normal heating at 60°C after 60min of dyeing, and (d) dyed cotton fiber using ultra sound at 30°C after 60min of dyeing.

Atomic force microscope three dimensional image of (a) untreated cotton fiber, (b) dyed cotton fiber using normal heating at 60°C after 60min of dyeing, and (c) dyed cotton fiber using ultra sonication at 30°C after 60min of dyeing.
Categorization of the experiment according to the energy source and dye bath temperature.
Polydispersity index of the dye solution at different stage of dyeing.
Characteristic absorption bands of undyed cotton and cotton dyed using normal heating and ultra sonication after 60min of dyeing.

row.entry: Cotton, cotton dyed at NH30, NH40
Cotton dyed at US 30, US 40, US 60, NH 60

head.ce:title: Ultrasound energy to accelerate dye uptake and dye–fiber interaction of reactive dye on knitted cotton fabric at low temperatures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sri Lanka Institute of Nanotechnology (SLINTEC), Nanotechnology & Science Park, Mahenwatta, Pitipana, Homagama, Sri Lanka

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Colombo, Colombo 03, Sri Lanka

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of Colombo, Colombo 03, Sri Lanka and Sri Lanka Institute of Nanotechnology (SLINTEC), Nanotechnology & Science Park, Mahenwatta, Pitipana, Homagama, Sri Lanka.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acoustic cavitation formed due to propagation of ultrasound wave inside a dye bath was successfully used to dye cotton fabric with a reactive dye at lower temperatures. The energy input to the system during sonication was 0.7W/cm2. This was within the energy range that contributes towards forming cavitation during ultra-sonication. The influence of ultrasound treatment on dye particle size and fiber morphology is discussed. Particle size analysis of the dye bath revealed ultra-sonication energy was capable of de-agglomeration of hydrolyzed dye molecules during dyeing. SEM micrograph and AFM topographical image of the fiber surface revealed fiber morphology remains unchanged after the sonication. The study was extended in understanding the contribution of ultrasound method of dyeing towards achieving good color strength on the fabric, compared to the normal heating method of dyeing. Study showed color strength obtained using ultra sound method of dyeing is higher compared to normal heating dyeing. Ultrasound energy was able to achieve the good color strength on cotton fabric at very low temperature such as 30°C, which was approximately 230% more than the color strength achieved in normal heating method of dyeing. This indicates that energy input to the system using ultrasound was capable of acting as an effective alternative method of dyeing knitted cotton fabrics with reactive dye.

ce:section.ce:para: As far as the textile industry is concerned, textile dyeing is an essential process step which imparts esthetic properties to the fabric. The dyeing of textile fibers is carried out in an aqueous solution at elevated temperatures and therefore common textile dyeing processes demand high consumption of water and energy. This causes relatively high degree of environmental pollution. It is well known that cotton fabrics are best dyed using reactive dyes as reactive dyes are capable of making covalent bonds with cellulose structures [1]. Over six decades, various investigations were carried out to ascertain the possibility of using alternative energy forming mechanisms and chemical pre-treatment methodologies in textile processes. This will help to reduce the processing time for dyeing, reduce the environmental pollution and most importantly to improve the quality of the product [2–10].

In the sound spectrum ultrasound frequency lies in the range of 20kHz to 10MHz which is further divided into power (20kHz to 2MHz) and diagnostic (5–10MHz) ultrasound [11,12]. Power ultrasound is of much important for a variety of chemical and physical processes, such as accelerated chemical reaction, emulsification, degassing and extraction [13–16]. These enhanced effect of ultrasound in the field of power ultrasound is due to the cavitation phenomena [17]. Acoustic cavitation is defined as the formation of gas-filled micro-bubbles or cavities in liquid once the pressure of the spot decreases below the saturation vapor pressure of the dissolved gas in the liquid [18,19]. The growth and collapse of these cavities (micro bubbles) under proper conditions create drastic local conditions (high temperature and pressure). Explosion of these acoustic cavitation near solid surfaces can generate micro-jets which facilitate the liquid to move with a velocity of up to 110m/s [20]. Ejected micro-jets are capable of degassing the textile surface and also de-agglomeration of the dye particles inside the dye bath [21–25]. In addition these micro jets are effectively involved in improving wet textile treatments, where mass transfer occurs in the inter- and intra-yarn pores of the textile [26].

Some researchers have reported the successful use of ultrasound for dyeing different types of fabrics (cotton, Nylon, Lycra, polyester, wool) leading to good color strength and fastness properties [27–30]. In addition to the improved properties for the textile, ultrasound assisted dyeing reported to have lowered the dyeing time and temperature. It has been reported that the dyeing of cationized cotton fabrics with natural dye using ultra-sonication managed to reduce the dyeing time by half [31]. The use of ultra sound resulted in good color strength and better wash fastness for dyeing of acrylic fibers using basic dyes and polyester/Lycra with three different types of reactive dyes [31,32]. The improvement in fastness property is attributed to the good covalent fixation of dye with the fabric, due to the better dye penetration into the fibers. Although the presence of ultrasound during dyeing has improved the properties of the dyeing process, it was identified that the ultrasound pre-treatment for the fabric or dyeing solution does not improve the fastness or color strength of the dyed samples [33]. An investigation of the pre-treated Nylon fabric using ultrasound revealed that the use of ultrasound increases the percentage of the fiber crystallinity which will retard the dye uptake. However enhanced effect of power ultrasound can overcome this phenomenon while dyeing the fabric using ultrasound. In one research study effect of ultra-sonication for the acceleration of reactive dye uptake in bamboo cellulose fabric was investigated. In the presence of ultra-sonication the color yield of the fabric was improved to 5–6% [34]. Ultra sonication was also able to reduce the dyeing time of cotton fabric using reactive dyes in cold pad batch dyeing process where the dyeing time was reduced to 8h compared to the conventional dyeing time of 12h [35]. The use of power ultrasound on cotton fabric and dye bath pre-treatment in continuous dyeing process, has led to improve the dye uptake while maintaining good exhaustion and fixation [36].

This research study has focused on analyzing the effects of ultrasound in dyeing of cotton fabric using a reactive dye. Reactive dyes can form covalent bonds between the fibers and the dye as shown in Fig. 1 (b) [37]. The reactive dye used in this study is classified as a hot dye where dye fiber reaction is commonly taking place at temperatures above 60°C [38]. However reactive dyes are prone to undergo undesirable hydrolysis in aqueous solution, resulting in decreasing the dye fixation which leads to the formation of dye agglomeration in the dye bath [39]. We hypothesize that the ultrasound can effectively breakdown the dye particles in the dye solution and provide a solution to the dye particle agglomeration which is prominent in the conventional dyeing process. Although the use of ultrasound in dyeing of cotton fabric using reactive dyes was investigated, to the best of our knowledge a little or no documentation on dye fiber interaction at relatively low temperature of dyeing was published. This study has specifically paid an attention to observe the dye fiber interaction at relatively low temperature utilizing the ultra sound energy. The dyeing was carried out at different controlled temperatures. Mass transfer (exhaustion) and fixation of dye under two dyeing conditions as normal heating and ultra-sonication were compared. This allowed us to evaluate the efficiency of the energy produced by ultra-sonication to normal heating for the dyeing process.

Scoured and bleached 100% knitted cotton fabrics (203g/m2) and Remazol yellow 3RS (YD) dye powder was received from Textured Jersey Pvt. Ltd. Sodium sulfate and sodium carbonate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All chemicals were used without further purification. De-ionized water was used for dyeing experiments.

Reactive yellow dye 1% on weight of fabric (owf) was dissolved in distilled water (1:20 material to liquor ratio). The dye mixture was stirred for 1h until the dye particles are fully dissolved in the distilled water.

Dye solution was transferred to the stainless steel vessel and bath temperature was maintained at the required running temperature using an external cooling unit. When ultra sound was used for dyeing, an ice bath was used to maintain the temperature. For normal heating dyeing procedure, the dye bath temperature was regulated using direct heating. Once the initial dye bath temperature reaches the required value, the fabric was added and the dyeing was continued for 30min. After 30min of dyeing a sample of dyed fabric and dye solution was taken from the dye bath to measure the exhaustion and fixation of the dye. At this stage sodium carbonate (10g/L) was added to the dye bath and continued dyeing for another 30min. After 1h of dyeing, a sample of dyed fabric and dye solution was taken from the dye bath to measure the exhaustion and fixation of the dye.

For ultra sound dyeing method an ultrasound generating probe was used, UP400S (Hielscher™) with the probe tip diameter of 1.3cm, with ultrasound intensity of 60%, at 430W power output at the frequency of 24kHz. Maximum reflection of sound waves inside the dye bath is essential to maximize the energy input to the dye bath during sonication. This was achieved by using a stainless steel vessel for dyeing. Water stainless steel interface has the ability to reflect 80% of sound energy which hits its surface. The experiment set up for ultrasound assisted dyeing is shown in Fig. 2 .

During the dyeing process dye bath temperature was maintained at the set temperature consciously using an external cooling mechanism as shown in Fig. 2. Dye bath temperature was maintained at 30°C, 40°C and 60°C for the normal heating and ultra-sonication method of dyeing.

Six different experiments were carried out to investigate the effect of ultra sound on textile dyeing over normal heating method of dyeing at different temperatures. Experimental methods and specific dyeing temperatures are presented in Table 1 . These numbering will be used in the results and discussion section to present results obtained during individual dyeing conditions.

Amount of dye exhaustion was measured using the absorbance of the dye solution at the maximum wavelength (438nm for yellow color of the dye) using a UV–VIS spectrophotometer (SU6600, SHIMADZU). At different stages of dyeing, 2ml of the dye liquid was taken out from the dye solution into a volumetric flask and diluted with distilled water. Diluted dye sample was placed in a glass cuvette and transmittance measurement mode was used. Dye bath maximum absorption wave length was selected at 438nm (as indicated using the arrow mark in Fig. 2 in Supplementary data) which corresponds to the yellow color of the dye.

Concentration of the dye solution was measured after reference to the calibration curve obtained using Beer–Lambert law to measure the absorbance. The amount of dye exhaustion was measured using the following equation:
(1)
E =
1 -
C
t
C
o

where,

ce:list-item.ce:para: C o =Concentration of the dye solution before dyeing,

Ct
=Concentration of the dye solution at the dyeing stage t.

ce:section.ce:para: At each stage of dyeing, a sample of dyed cotton fabric was taken out. These dyed cotton fabrics were first washed with cold water in a bath of liquor ratio 40:1 using 4g/L non-ionic detergent for 30min. Finally the sample was rinsed at 95°C with hot water in a bath of liquor ratio 40:1, and then dried at ambient temperature. Color strength of theses dyed cotton fabrics was measured using the K/S value obtained using the UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Dyed and washed fabric sample was placed on a microscope glass slide using double scotch tape. The diffuse reflectance measurement mode was used in 380–800nm light range. Untreated normal cotton fabric was used as the baseline for the measurements.

K/S value of the dyed fabric can be defined using the following equation.
(2)
F =
K
S
= ( 1 -
R
2
) / 2 R
where,

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy input to the dye bath during sonication
ce:section.ce:para:
Energy input to the dye bath during sonication can be calculated using a calorimetric method. When mass (mi ) and heat capacity (Ci ) of the dye bath are known, ultrasound energy input (Ei ) to the bath can be calculated using the following equation [6].
(3)
E
i
=
dT
dt
∗
∑
m
i
C
i

Temperature profile of the dye bath with time due to the ultra-sonication was obtained (Supplementary data Fig. 1). Initial slope of this curve indicates the effectiveness of the ultrasound in raising dye bath temperature.
dT/dt is the initial slope (at t =0) of the temperature graph of the ultrasound bath.

Dynamic light scattering of the dye solution at each stage of dyeing was performed at 20°C using a Malvern Zetasizer NanoZs compact scattering spectrophotometer with a 4.0mW He–Ne laser (633nm wavelength) at a scattering angle of 170°.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical characterization of the cotton fabrics
ce:section.ce:para: Atomic Force Microscopic (AFM) analysis of cotton fabric and dyed cotton fabric was performed using a Park System AFM. AFM images were captured using an XE-100 microscope. The measurements were performed in air at room temperature using non-contact mode, with Si tips of the 1650-00 type scanning at a rate of 0.5Hz.
The morphology of the surface of cotton fabric and dyed cotton fabric was characterized using a Hitachi SU6600 field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM).

Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectra of cotton and cotton dyed at different conditions, were obtained using Bruker, Vertex 80. Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR) measurement mode was used with the sample mounted on a ZnSe crystal. Absorption spectra were recorded in 32 scans in the 600–4000cm−1 wave number range with 4cm−1 resolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy input to the dye bath during sonication
ce:section.ce:para: The intensity of ultrasound energy in the dye bath directly influences the formation of cavitation inside the bath which will drive the dye particles into the cotton fabric. Generally ultrasound intensity of 0.3–10W/cm2 can produce cavitation bubbles in a liquid medium [17,18]. Therefore an initial study was carried out to measure the energy released to the dye system during ultra-sonication. Using Eq. (3), energy input to the dye bath was calculated as 0.7W/cm2 which is in the desirable limit for producing cavitation inside the dye bath.
The percentage of dye exhaustion from dye bath was calculated according to the method described in Section 2.4 and is presented in Fig. 3 .

The energy produce by ultra sound is capable of achieving 84% dye exhaustion at 30°C after 60min of dyeing. However when normal heating method was used, only 60% of dye exhaustion can be achieved even after 60min of dyeing at 30°C. In order to increase the dye exhaustion for normal heating method of dyeing, the dyeing temperature or dyeing time should be increased. At 60°C the maximum dye exhaustion value obtained for normal heating was only 77% and it was 8% below the value obtained for ultra sound dyeing. Dye exhaustion was at a maximum value of 84% when bath temperature was regulated even at a lower temperature of 30°C when ultra sonication energy was presented in the dye bath
Fabrics were measured for its color strength value (K/S) as described in Section 2.5. Fig. 4 shows the comparison of K/S of the dyed cotton fabric at each stage of the dyeing process.

The energy produced using ultrasound was able to achieve a color depth value of 4.46 at 30°C which was 235% higher than the color depth obtained for the fabric dyed using normal heating during first 30min of dyeing. After dyeing for 1h at 60°C the ultra sound method of dyeing was able to achieve a color depth of 5.68 which was a 16% increment compared to normal heating method.

Addition of sodium carbonate to the dye bath after 30min of dyeing has improved the color strength of the fabric when dyed using normal heating at 30°C. Sudden increment in the dye uptake can be due to the added sodium carbonate which act as an electrolyte for altering the ionic potential between dye molecule and cotton fibers allowing more dye molecules to interact with the cotton fibers [40–42]. To further explore the dye fiber reaction mechanism the FT-IR analysis was carried out for the dyed fabrics, dyed at different temperatures. When using ultra sound even without the addition of sodium carbonate more dye uptake has taken place during the first 30min of dyeing. Sonication has allowed opening of the intra yarn space through degassing which allowed more dye penetration without the addition of sodium carbonate. The morphology of the yarn surface was analyzed using SEM and AFM to observe inhomogeneity of the yarn surface due to ultra sound. Also high speed water jets formed during explosion of cavities can drive dye molecules to intra and inter yarn surface of the fabric during ultra sonication dyeing.

According to the results obtained in Figs. 3 and 4 it is evident that under normal heating conditions reactive dyes should be dyed at relatively higher temperatures for longer time in order to achieve the higher color strength and dye exhaustion. Reactive dyes are characteristic for their unfavorable hydrolysis in aqueous medium at elevated temperatures for a prolonged time period. This will adversely affect the dye uptake and fixation of dye molecules on the yarn surface. This phenomenon can be minimized if dyeing can be performed at low temperatures for shorter time period where hydrolysis of the dye molecules is controlled.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dye particle size analysis during dyeing
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrolysis of the dye molecule inside dye bath can be characterized by dye particle agglomeration inside the dye bath. The change of particle size of the dye molecule due to ultra sound was analyzed using particle size analyzer at different stages of dyeing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of dye fiber interaction at different stages of dyeing
ce:section.ce:para: Hydroxyl group of the cellulose acquires a slight negative charge in aqueous medium as illustrated in schematic representation in Fig. 6 (a). Reactive dye used in this study has an anionic character due to the water soluble sulfonate groups on the dye molecule [42]. Therefore negative charge of the cellulose ion is undesirable for dye fiber interaction. Addition of sodium carbonate electrolyte after 30min of dyeing will neutralize the negative charge of the cellulose ion and slight positive charge will be acquired by the cotton yarn surface (Fig. 6b). This will promote more dye fiber interaction and resulted in more dye exhaustion. This phenomenon is further illustrated in schematic representation in Fig. 6(a) and (b). When normal heating is used covalent bonding of the dye and fiber (between Cl− of the dye molecule and Na+ of the cellulose ion) will take place at relatively high temperatures. Due to this phenomenon during normal heating method of dyeing it is suggested that penetration and dye fiber interaction will happen at elevated temperatures after the addition of electrolytes. In contrast during ultra sonication method of dyeing dye fiber interaction will happen at low temperatures such as 30°C. FT-IR spectra of the dyed fabrics at different stages of dyeing were obtained to support the suggested mechanism of dye fiber interaction. Dyed fabrics were subjected for washing as described in Section 2.5 before analysis to remove unbound dye molecules.

SEM micrograph of the normal fiber (Fig. 8 a) shows parallel ridges and grooves with twisted convolution. Magnified image of this fiber surface (Fig. 8b) indicates the distance between these ridges and grooves are in the micrometer scale. After the fabric was subjected for dyeing using normal heating yarn surface is covered by the dye coating (Fig. 8c) which is unique to the untreated cotton fiber surface. Cotton fabric dyed using normal heating and ultra sonication (Fig. 7b and c) shows a deposition of a uniform coating on the yarn surface which covers a certain percentage of naturally occurring parallel ridges and grooves. Other than the coating deposited on fiber surface Fig. 8(b) and (c) indicate fiber morphology remain uniform and undamaged after being subjected for dyeing using normal heating or ultra sonication. High energy water jets produce during sonication has allowed increasing only the intra fiber gaps temporally during the dyeing process to penetrate dye molecules at low temperatures of dyeing. For closer examination of the fiber surface topographical images of the yarn surface was obtained using non-contact mode AFM scanned at 0.5Hz.

Topographical image of the untreated cotton fiber surface clearly show the ridges and grooves on the fiber surface (Fig. 9 a). Subjected for dyeing using normal heating or ultra sound these naturally occurring parallel lines of the cotton fiber has smoothened out. A coating on the fiber surface is visible in Fig. 9(b) and (c). Although the coating thickness was not evaluated using these images, the dye molecules deposited on the fiber surface was able to cover ridges and grooves which are in the micrometer range of height. Parallel lines on the fiber surface was replaced with a coating which can be observed as deposited spherical agglomerates in normal heating dyed fabric (Fig. 8b). For ultra sound dyed fabric the coating was applied as a film on the surface with less spherical particle deposition according to the information obtained using AFM micrograph (Fig. 8c).

The energy released to the dye bath with the explosion of cavities during the ultra-sonication, was able to drive dye particle into the fabric more effectively. Further the de-agglomeration of dye particles in the dye bath was facilitated, resulting a more homogenous dye bath and lesser particle size of the dye. This phenomenon allowed high dye absorption into the fabric with a uniform fixation throughout the fabric surface. Ultra sound dyeing was able to achieve 235% more color strength to the fabric at a very low temperature of 30°C compared to normal heating dyeing. The use of power ultrasound during dyeing can allow better fixation of reactive dyes categories at lower temperatures. As ultrasound method of dyeing leads to more dye exhaustion compared to conventional method of dyeing, ultra sound method can be used as a new approach to reduce the dye wastage during the dyeing process, and hence can be considered as a more environment friendly method of dyeing of cotton fabric using reactive dyes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Textured Jersey Pvt. Ltd., Sri Lanka for providing the fabric samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2015.10.002.
